description:
## Background

Workspace persistence is not a simple preference write. A valid candidate must remain retryable after a later load failure, while a failed final save must never leave in-memory or on-disk state that a future save accidentally promotes to Current. Concurrent selections, retries, and removal can otherwise interleave and resurrect a stale or removed Workspace. Real desktop acceptance also needs isolated persistent storage so tests never read or overwrite a developer catalog.

## What to build

Build a backend-owned, transactional Workspace state and persistence service. It owns the versioned Workspace Catalog, optional Current Workspace, Pending Workspace, request generation, storage health, and an injectable store location for tests. Every observable catalog/current transition is serialized and becomes visible only after its required persistence save succeeds.

## Technical Approach

- Use one versioned persistent catalog schema containing remembered root paths and the optional last successful Current Workspace. Cap it at 100 entries and retain exact-path identity under ordinary path handling; do not introduce symlink alias deduplication.
- Serialize all catalog, current, pending, retry, and removal mutations through one backend service/critical section. A proposed transition is built from a stable snapshot, saved explicitly, and only then committed to service-visible state.
- If a store save fails after mutation, restore or discard the pending store mutation before another operation can save it. A failed switch must not be resurrected by a later unrelated catalog save.
- Removing a Workspace while it is Pending invalidates that request generation. Removing Current Workspace also clears it; neither a stale load nor a later retry may republish the removed target without a new deliberate selection.
- Treat malformed, unsupported, or unreadable persisted catalog data as a storage-health failure. Do not silently overwrite it. Surface a recoverable state that allows retry and an explicit user-requested reset of Beadsmith local Workspace memory; resetting never touches repositories or Beadwork data.
- Keep the store backend-owned. Production resolves its normal application-data location; Rust, integration, and real-desktop tests receive a fresh, isolated temporary store location through a supported test-only app configuration. The test harness must never write a store file directly or use a developer store.

## Considerations

- A validated candidate can be persisted as known before loading; Current Workspace and MRU promotion are a separate final commit after full load.
- Store health is distinct from repository validation and Issue Explorer load health. UI callers need typed states so they can present the correct recovery action.
- This service owns mutation ordering. The later switch UI must not emulate ordering or persistence rules in React.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Catalog/current state uses a versioned, backend-owned schema with explicit save semantics and a 100-entry cap.
- [ ] Failed saves cannot leave partial in-memory or durable state that a later write publishes accidentally.
- [ ] Concurrent selection, retry, and removal operations are serialized; removal invalidates a matching pending request.
- [ ] Storage corruption or unsupported schema is recoverable without silently deleting user data, and an explicit reset affects only Beadsmith local Workspace memory.
- [ ] Test runs use unique isolated storage through supported application configuration and never touch developer catalog data.
- [ ] The service exposes typed state suitable for the later TauRPC and selector layers without exposing the store itself.

## Test Plan

- Rust state-service tests for proposed-save-commit sequencing, rollback after save failure, cap eviction, exact-path re-add, generation invalidation, and removal during pending work.
- Corrupt/unsupported-store and explicit-reset tests proving repositories are never changed.
- Integration tests using isolated temporary storage and proving no state leaks across test cases.
- Run Rust tests, typed-RPC binding checks, and the test harness isolation check before closing.
title: Make Workspace persistence transactional and test-isolated
